context: Current closing price of Target (TGT): $117.88 USD as of 2026-04-13.
justification_text:
My point estimates reflected a modest upward drift from what I assumed was the prevailing price level at the time of the forecast. At the one-day horizon, the estimate of 118.5 was anchored heavily on recent price momentum and short-term technical levels, with very little deviation expected over such a brief window. Moving to one week at 119.5 and one month at 121.0, I applied a small positive drift assumption, consistent with a mild bullish bias likely derived from recent trend direction, mean-reversion tendencies, or a slightly positive carry or fundamental backdrop. The incremental increases across horizons are deliberately modest because I did not want to extrapolate aggressively without strong directional conviction.

The confidence interval widths reflect the natural scaling of uncertainty with time. At one day, the 90% interval of 113 to 124 captures roughly normal daily volatility, implying an annualized volatility assumption somewhere in the moderate range typical for a liquid financial instrument. By one week, the interval widens to 108–131, reflecting that multiple sessions of price movement compound uncertainty considerably. At one month, the interval of 98–145 acknowledges that over roughly 20 trading days, a wide variety of macro, fundamental, and sentiment shifts can move prices substantially in either direction. The asymmetry is roughly symmetric around the point estimates, suggesting I did not have strong skew conviction at the time.

Several specific risks could push outcomes outside these stated intervals. On the downside, a sudden macro shock such as an unexpected central bank policy shift, a credit event, or a geopolitical escalation could easily breach the lower bounds, particularly at the one-month horizon. On the upside, a strong earnings surprise, a short squeeze, or a sudden improvement in risk appetite could drive prices above 145. Liquidity conditions matter too — in thin markets, gaps can be violent and render even wide confidence intervals insufficient.

It is also worth acknowledging that these intervals carry a calibration caveat. The 90% confidence intervals are model-derived and depend heavily on volatility assumptions that themselves are uncertain. If realized volatility turns out to be significantly higher than the implied assumption — which can happen during regime changes — the true uncertainty is wider than stated. Conversely, a particularly quiet, range-bound environment could make even the one-month interval look overly wide in hindsight.
